Welcome everybody. This is the Q&A call for the traffic engine. It was May 6, 2021. I'm stunned that nobody asked any questions about iOS 14.5. I thought that was a really tired topic. Oh, for real. Yeah, I figured that would be the dominant one. Yeah, that's what I thought too. It's got a funny to think about. Nobody asked, but I'll sort of share a couple of thoughts on iOS 14.5, just sort of a starting point. And these are just a few things to clarify if you're doing anything in Facebook in the traffic engine and you heard mention of it, you're kind of wondering what in the world what's really going on here. So here's the challenge. About 80% of Facebook's traffic is mobile and 60% of that mobile traffic is on the iPhone. So Apple has recently released essentially a prompt. And in that prompt, you have to now opt into having apps share your tracking data. In the past, the iPhone allowed Facebook to use a phone number lookup system. And that was done in the background by default. And now it's not, now people are having to opt in and there's some estimate that maybe half of the people who get that prompt will say no. I think it's closer to like rounded up to a whole number, like 100% of the people are going to say no. I mean, just as soon as I saw the prompt, I was like, I would never say yes to this. So what that means at the end of the day is about 20 to 50% of your Facebook performance data isn't going to be reported accurately. That's just, that's the bottom line. So, you know, now we have this, there's sort of, there's three things that have been going on. So what did we think was going to happen? What actually happened when this was released about, I guess, is last Monday. And then what are the ramifications? And we still don't really know the ramifications. And there's still some dispute about what has actually happened. So just mentioning this now, just to sort of orient this all to this, the sky is kind of falling in many ways with Facebook. There will, things are going to evolve. And really, I also want to clarify this. All this means is people are opting out of being tracked on the iPhone. They're not opting out of seeing ads. It's like a, I've heard people are freaking out, like, you know, what's going to happen when nobody sees my ads on the iPhone? That's not what this is about. This is about their performance being tracked. So I think I'm going to do a, I may do it as like a missing chapter, or I may think come at it as some other way to, once I have, there's a broader understanding of what it means, we will probably just integrate it into the content, the traffic engine as well. It's just, we're still at that phase where we're going to have a month or two before we actually see what's really going on here. And we know some things that have happened. You know, Facebook has changed the attribution windows and they've done other things that are very significant. You know, for example, we can't retarget opted out users, you know, people who, we can't use our data for look like audiences. Like that's, this is very significant, but we still need to see how it all plays out before we start making some sweeping recommendations. All right, let's jump into questions. Get this out of my way. All right. So first question that was asked, I'll just read the, in general, I'll read the questions as written. Sometimes I'll summarize them. And it'll make sense why I do that. And then once the questions asked, I'll initially throw out an answer and then we can get Lauren and Andre contributing as well. Really hard questions. I'll just have Lauren and Andre answer. That's my strategy. All right, here's the question. As part of writing my own TTV, a durable business manifesto, I reread your paid traffic manifesto. Simple truth number seven struck a chord with me as I'm struggling to understand why I'm getting good levels of engagement with my peeps, but not so much conversion, or rather not as much as I wish I had. My question is, how do you identify the weakest link in your system? How do you troubleshoot a business system? I'm so curious to have your perspective on this. So just to refresh everybody's memory, because I know that you're sitting there with your copies of the paid traffic engine manifesto, of course. Simple truth number seven, there's only one way to improve your results. I'll just read parts of this very quickly. It's short. When we accept that our businesses are systems, we realize that they're subject to the insights of systems theory, perhaps the most profound insight is this one from LA Goldrat's theory of constraints. The only way to improve the throughput of a system is to improve the performance of its weakest link throughput, just so we're all clear. I should have defined that better when I wrote the manifesto throughput is the rate at which your system produces its primary output. So happy customers is the way we measure throughput, the way Andra and I measure throughput. So the rate at which we produce happy customers is our throughput. All right, in Goldrat's terminology, we need to focus on improving the system optimum, not improving a local optima and prove the whole don't take you with the parts. Layman's term, that means there's always one and only one place to focus our attention to improve our results. We just need to know where to look and then act accordingly. Okay, so what does that mean? So we have to take a step back to answer this question. The first thing we need to be very clear about is what are you optimizing for? Because we need to know how we're going to define throughput. And we need to do that with clarity. We can't say, and so that could be something as simple as I'm optimizing for sales. Okay, that that's clear. But and we need that level of clarity. So and then once we have that level of clarity, we need to then take the next step and say, okay, what is our current reality? What is the rate at which we're producing sales? And that could be in a week, that could be in a month, that could be a 90 day, whatever it is. But we need to we need to understand what the system is doing right now. And oftentimes, it's helpful to compare what the system is doing right now to some point in the past. You know, when I ran an agency, one of the things that I would often do is compare that whatever unit of period of time I was looking at for now with a client to the same period of time last year, or the previous several years, because that eliminates seasonality and lots of other factors. If you don't have that data, and if that data isn't meaningful for any variety of reasons, like you made a complete, you know, overhaul to your business, then you might need to compare say this week to last week. But if you haven't really changed anything, your ad spend is the same, the system is the same, then, you know, comparing one week to the next may not be as valuable as comparing say two to four weeks. You know, what you're trying to get a sense of is what's what's happening in this slice of time? I wouldn't go too far because at some point time and seasonality and other factors do affect the data. And that leads to another question, which is what data do you have? Do you if it's just if you're just sitting there, you know, you have a whiteboard, you're like, okay, I'm gonna kind of understand current reality here and whiteboard just has, you know, I spent X number of dollars, I got X number of clicks, as reported by the media platform, which could be wildly inflated. Then I got X number of leads, and I got X number of sales, like that if that's the data you have, that's the data you have. But you need to get it for me, at least I need to get it all out where I can see it. And I need and I tend to I want to I want to put the data in some sort of order that makes sense. Now, it may it sounds like what I'm doing is a linear analysis, which we rail about all the time. And that's not true. What I'm doing is I'm just seeing what's happening. Because the next thing that I have to ask myself is, do I really understand this data? And that means a lot of different things. So for example, I've seen this happen more times than I could count, unfortunately, which is somebody runs an ad for video views, and they see the first piece of data in Facebook, and it says you had, and let's say it's a seven minute video that goes to a landing page where they can opt in. And they look at the Facebook data, and it says, you know, 1500 video views. And then they look at their landing page data, and it says 10 leads. And they immediately do the quick calculation thinking that that's never going to work. What they don't realize is that they don't realize how Facebook defines a video view. And that what they really want to know is what how many people see all of the video, because I've seen this happen over and over again, that they that people assume the data means something that it doesn't mean they assume in this example, the data means that 1500 people watch their entire video. Whereas what it probably means is 1500 people of them 1450 had autoplay on the video started going and only what they really want to look at is you know what was the 50% here what was a 95% that's what I would really care about like who actually watched this video. And then what percentage of them went on to the landing page to become leads, you have to really dig in this. This sounds like I'm almost avoiding the question, like how do you find the weakest link, but you have to find the real story that your data is telling. And this is the part that gets overlooked over and over and over again, which is, you know, there is it's like well here's the data and it's in a spreadsheet. Who cares about that? What I care about is what is the data telling me? What story could I could I tell another person about this data? That's true, that the data tells me that confirms or are there things that the data disconfirms and you have to get that understanding first. I like to get all of that out of my head onto something because I'm very visual, Andre and I are both very visual, so I like to get that out of my head so I can you know what I say and I think about you know I stand back and look at it. I literally stand back and look at it, put on a whiteboard, I need to be able to walk around, sometimes I stare at it for a while and I leave it and you know I'm trying to think about what could happen but from that ideas begin to emerge. The first thing that's likely to happen is what you think is true about what's going on is not at all true. So that's the first thing we need is precision and clarity. What's actually happening. How many people, you know, how many people, whatever it was, assume it's a text-based ad and that ad then goes to a landing page and that landing page has let's say a thousand words of content and then an opt-in. Okay and then there are x number of emails and an opportunity to convert at the end and that whole thing takes 14 days or so. Like we need a period of time where we have enough data in that system to understand what actually happened and from that we're going to see some things emerge. One thing that might emerge is generally problems generally come in, in my experience they generally come in two buckets. Bucket number one is traffic, bucket number two is conversion. Like those are the two big buckets so that's where I start looking. So traffic problems show up as well. I get a lot of people and there's not, there's not always a clear delineation but if you have an ad that's say really curiosity driven and you're getting tons of clicks on the ad but nobody, and then you look at the bounce rate for your landing page or the time spent on your landing page is really low and then you notice that your resolving the curiosity early. And we see this happen a lot you know and the ad opens up there's some bit of curiosity and then it gets resolved in like the first paragraph. So people scratch their itch and what that shows up as then is like you know very low, a short time on site, lots of clicks. So you can, that tells, it's telling you a story. They're like oh okay I'm getting a lot of curiosity clicks. I've answered the curiosity nobody needs to go. Nobody needs to move forward. This shows up in e-commerce too. I had a client years ago where one of their golf retailer and one of the companies that they sold didn't allow their prices to be displayed on a website. They had to be displayed in the cart dumbest thing in the world, absolutely dumbest thing in the world. So what would happen is it looked like their cart abandonment was way higher than it actually was. People were just price checking. So you need to know the story your data is telling. So once you know that story then this is where you're in general you're going to find something that and it's probably the data is not going to point to it. In a linear analysis the assumption is the data will tell you like it's like okay you got a thousand clicks and then a hundred people opted in and of those hundred ten purchased and they're like okay so if I put you know if I could get another thousand people and like that's a linear analysis and what a systems analysis says instead is like what's what's what's going on here in total and what's what do I think and this is what this is the hardest word. This is the most difficult concept people want to hear when it comes to an analysis like this is think you have to step step back and you have to think about what could be causing this and then that's where you form a hypothesis and then ask yourself is this can I do is this defensible do I look at the data that I have and make the case that this is true and does the date is there any data that I have that could disconfirm that this is true like it really is that complicated and I don't I don't want to suggest that there's it's sort of magic when you read Ellie Goldrat's book you start with a goal it's a it's a novel but it's incredibly valuable but you'll realize that there's work in finding the constraint and the constraint moves once you once you address a constraint well it it shifts someplace else so the big constraints tend to be the traffic source isn't that great another constraint tends to be that you're you're providing the wrong incentives in your ad that you're you're opening so many loops and you're generating so much curiosity in the ad that somebody wants to click on it to find out what's next that's often a very big constraint there another constraint is that you're making a promise of lots of free goodies and those free goodies are so attractive that somebody is willing to just give a throwaway email address or their email address and unsubscribe because they want the goodies but they don't it's they don't really care about the offer that follows or they you've generated just enough curiosity for the the goodies that you're offering but they don't they don't really need anything after that you didn't really get their their sustained attention what you got was a sort of a sugary attention where it's like oh that report looks good I could use that I answer the question I'll save it on my desktop with all the other things I've downloaded and never read like that's that's another place to look for it or there's also like the lack of congruence so that you you have instead of having a single thematic arc where your ad relates to your landing page relates to how you build relationships relates to how you sell that it's disjointed and when it's disjointed that's actually the constraint it's an underlying constraint it's a conceptual constraint it's not one that you see in the data it's just one that shows up where nothing seems to work well together and the reason it doesn't work well together is because it's not a whole thing it's a bunch of parts and this is really how and I don't want to get on my soapbox and start railing here it's it's like 8 20 in the morning and I'll get super animated before I've had enough coffee but the this is the thing that pisses me off to no end is it what people are selling here comes a soapbox now I can tell my heart rate's getting is increasing what people are selling online so much is bullshit it's this idea if you do this part really well then the whole thing is going to get better and that's just not true I spent the best example is lead gen it is really easy to generate cheap leads and you can find an infinite number of systems to generate and courses that how to generate cheap leads but no businesses aren't built on cheap leads businesses are built on customers so when you the things that you do to get cheap leads often undermine everything that follows like you know big promises a lot to lead magnets stuff like pile on the bonuses you know for signing up and you do all of that and you completely undermine everything that follows and then people scratch their head and wonder why doesn't my emails convert because you've got people in with the wrong incentives so making a consistent incentive structure which is I have something valuable to share with people who have these desires these needs these problems to solve and I'm not going to bribe you to do that the the bribe the underlying value is the fact that I I've identified for you this issue that you might have and I'm going to show you how you can solve it that's it if someone responds to that well that's excellent but if it's you pile on 5000 bonuses to get them to hand over the email you're you're pushing pushing pushing rather than letting the value that you've created pull so that's a very long answer the underlying message is there is no formula for this you have to be so intimately familiar with your data and the story that the data is telling that when you do something you sense what happens in the data after and and it's either what you expected or what you didn't that's how you confirm and disconfirm and it's very much a process of saying like I understand what's going on in my business I understand how the parts all work together I'm going to do this thing and if this thing makes a positive difference here's how I think it'll show up if it doesn't if it makes a negative or neutral difference then this is what won't happen and then I'll sort of know that thing didn't really contribute much it's or didn't contribute at all or I've taken a step backwards accidentally then you have to explain yourself a little how how could that have happened that's I'm if I can externalize my thinking I'll just think I'd like for for a second yes so one of the things you mentioned is you know we we look at all of this through through the lens of systems thinking and in systems thinking there's an idea called synthesis which is which is really the opposite of analysis so analysis is taking something complex and breaking down into the tiny little parts and then looking at this parts in isolation of the system where synthesis is about understanding the whole and the parts at the same time and the relationships and connections that make up the dynamics of the whole so they they're very different so with that idea set to a side there's another idea which is when you're looking at anything in isolation you don't have there are certain things that you don't this in from my experience you know working with 220 plus clients over the years the almost all of the leverage is in the offer and how it's expressed and how it matches up with an audience's needs when you get that right everything's easy when you get that wrong nothing is easy and it's like that's where all the leverage is so some if the if you have a system that's just for whatever reason it's just not producing the results that you want look at the offer first and really and ask yourself does this is am I talking about things that are keeping my prospects up at night is this something that may be fine to have or that or what I'm giving them is kind of enough because it's not that big of a desire or am I really speaking to these deep visceral core emotional needs because once you do is where and actually let me take an even a bigger step back this is where when I when I created the traffic triangle I actually created that or the traffic pyramid I created that sort of on the fly in an answer to a question like this at a an event I was speaking and we're trying and someone's trying to figure out like where's all the traffic and so I came up with these three categories of traffic the people who are looking for you specifically and you could be you as a person or you as if you have a named system so if somebody searches for Andre by name or searches for auto responder madness by name that's a very high quality pool of traffic and it's very and it's low volume relatively low volume that's the the tip of the pyramid the next layer below that you know people who are looking for what you have to offer so that could be that that's where things get broader it could it's it's how people might look for you without knowing about you so you might you know say email learn email marketing if they're looking for auto responder madness or story based email marketing or what like whatever it is like they're they're not looking for Andre specifically or for the course specifically but they're looking for the the they're actively looking you're searching for what that course offers that's the second pool generally higher quality or it's still high quality traffic very competitive not often not enormous amounts of quality of quality then there's this last pool and this this last pool I've you know I've talked about is that these are people who if they knew you or what you had to offer exists might be interested and that sounds like it's just something I made up on the fly but it's it's it's the most precise example I can I can give for the problem that Mike is facing and it's a problem many of us face which is there are people out there who if they just knew this value that I've created existed they'd want it but they're not looking for it they're not searching now sometimes that means they're not actually interested in it but a lot of times it means they don't even know what to look for they don't even know that that help exists and that's the you know in the past the Google display network was really the only way that we could approximate that traffic but because of banner blindness and other things it was very difficult so you know the the way we would do it in the past is we might find an article that was about a certain subject and that that that website you know allowed Google display network advertising so then you think oh somebody's reading this article and they see an ad for my stuff which is I think is it would appeal to someone who would be interested in this article like that was the weirdness that we used to do and it worked in a back when traffic was cheap then Facebook came along and Facebook is the system that finds that enormous pool of potential traffic at the bottom and what generally happens you know that Facebook is so popular is that no one goes through this process that Mike went through which is really the way the traffic engine is laid out that yes absolutely start with active intent with search because it's the quality of someone who is actively looking for you is almost always going to when you if you if you look for every hundred you know prospects the people who are actively looking are going to be far more valuable than the people who are sort of passively bump into it that's we can just all agree that makes sense but what happens is you know people often hear like oh I need to do Facebook advertising so they go right to Facebook and they skip the part to find out are there actually people looking for this already Mike did the work and realized that it doesn't the thing that he's offering is is very specialized and it's unlikely that there's going to be enough traffic there to sustain a business because they're just the people just aren't looking for it there's they know there's a problem but they don't even know how to think about who might be able to help and solve the problem so that then suggests that Mike had in all of us who are in similar always fun I learned more on these Q&A calls anybody else does there's an interesting couple and this gets back to the first question this is a it's funny this is a perfect example of systemic effects we had a question asked earlier another question asked later the two don't seem related in any ways yet they point to this emergent idea that now I hope I can articulate as I really set myself up so when you when we're looking at something holistically at a systems level you can think about a couple of in andre just said this a couple of different ways that we can approach solving quote unquote a problem right so if we have lots of unsubscribes we're kind of making an implicit decision which is we're making the doorway to the lead a little wider knowing that we're going to lose people over the email series or we could narrow the door more at the beginning and we would expect to see fewer people unsubscribe over time like that's a systemic emergent property that it's a really good example of how to think about like think about your business on the systems view and but that requires that you you take this step back a little bit and say okay you know I'm getting a lot of leads but those but those leads are not converting or we'll just use this example I'm getting a lot of leads but over the course of a really long email see you know 20 plus emails very long campaign I lose 15 percent well okay am I losing like now it's a matter of like trying to figure out the story here am I losing 15 percent because I I've made some I've opened the door in the beginning wider so that people who are probably looking for things like how much does this cost and they're they're looking for information I that I'm attractive to them but they're going to opt out by the end like that's a possibility you say okay well how would I test that well one way you can test it is you could you could narrow the entry point you could you have sort of qualify the traffic a little more what would what you would expect to see happen if you did that is you would have fewer people unsubscribe you know if you narrow the door even more or if you narrow the the entry point and you still get the same number of unsubscribes well that's puzzling that's not what you expected to happen so then then you start asking yourself questions like well how how could those two things be true right and that's that's that's really the game right it's not it's not really the data as much as the data it's it's and it's not just it's it's the data and yes it's the story but you you kind of have to know what you think is happening and why even if you're wildly wrong like there's there's a story I just happened to reread it last night Michael Lewis's new book so it's it's fresh on my mind but there's and I don't I have no idea if this story is true or if it's apocryphal it doesn't really matter but the way the way this I've heard the story a million times and it's actually connected to this question because I map but just the just having a map gave them the confidence to find their way home or was completely lucky right so like sometimes just having a narrative gets you started even if the narrative is based on what you did and what you understand of reality like the now your narrative doesn't match and you know science is just a continuing attempt to get to have our narrative more closely match like you know Andre and I are writers we love the written word we send yeah we have a we have a long ad that sends people to a lot of content on a landing page and then it's time to opt in and they're like yeah maybe not they're not quite there one thing we could do we wouldn't do this because of and I'll explain why we wouldn't do this but one thing we could do is we could think well we've actually had some feedback from our last workshop a lot of people seem to like the video screencast overview thing so maybe there's a percentage of our audience potential audience out there that would respond better to video so we could reach we could the hypothesis would be that by changing the medium from written text to video we might attract that 15 20% and whatever it is of our of our potential audience that responds more to video so then we would retarget people you know leads who stayed a certain amount of time but didn't convert and we would retarget them and then have sort of the equivalent messaging that we had in our written work in a video I mean we're having a conversation about things and then and we would expect that you know maybe some percentage of those people would respond to that now the reason we don't do that is we don't really offer a lot of video content it's it's rare in our courses so we we don't want to attract people but you know we don't want to put out the wrong bait and say okay yeah come in you know come on in we here's a video we know we now know that you prefer video and oh by the way if we ever sell you anything there's a very little video in it like that that's that's just incongruent that's a systemic consideration like we're looking at our whole business system and saying yes we could find a way to to improve the local optima we could get an extra say 15 20% of our leads we could recapture by showing them video but we know at the system level we're eventually going to to make those people unhappy because and we're optimizing for happy customers and we're not going to make them happy because we brought them in the video knowing we don't do video like this is that's a really great example of making a systemic consideration that we could boost at the local level and probably undermine our results at the overall system level so and I feel like I'm dancing around the retarget but what I want you to hear is how I think about this question more than because there is no there's no actual answer to this question it entirely depends on the source of traffic how you're presenting the information the where in the process that traffic is in their own mind are they are they rated to sign on the dotted line for installer they just want to compare prices are they thinking about like that's all expressed in search intent but I would out so my advice is answer all of those questions first then ask yourself are you driving that traffic to the right step based on their search intent this is why it's very having to say but in reality like they may be checking their phone at a red light they may be checking their phone for 10 seconds before they they rush off to do something else where we live in a busy distracted world and the reality is sometimes we we may be a perfect fit for that person and they may be interested but for whatever reason they got distracted so a lot of times retargeting is just this is somewhere in the traffic engine because it sort of came to me as a revelation it said Zen parable you can never step in the same stream twice that's that's less true for for Google ads you know when someone is expressing the same search intent they're sort of like a daily inventory of people looking for the same things and I think yes there's certainly context effects that but in general I think that is a that's more of a constant I think that's a little more reliable so when you a b test in Google ads with highly focused search intent I think the a b testing is fairly reliable I don't think that's true with Facebook Lauren and I have had some really in-depth conversations about this and he has convinced me and I've continued to convince myself that Facebook is a after the or the day the Saturday after the the elections in the United States when Joe Biden was declaring the winner of the election and the Facebook ad you saw that day if you were like an ardent Trump supporter the Facebook ad you saw that day probably landed with you differently than one that you saw two weeks earlier two weeks later same as true if you were a Biden supporter right you you were doing an ad a b test and it included the weeks after you know like the period of time after the US election and I tried to compare that to the same period of time the year before that's that's just not an accurate test context matters so much and face it because Facebook isn't is engineered context you know what we think of a you know people believe their news feed represents the news and know one is a more right-leaning news source the other is a left-leaning news source so your your your feed's going to change if you depending on what like the your reality changes so I think from a is a very typical this is this is what I would expect to see so I suggest everybody click on the chart so you can see it all kind of give you the highlights here so this is a four step process four pages 1.8 was it 1.8 thousand so 1800 1800 and 10 it looks like people see the first page and then 556 C have made it to the last page so around 30 percent a little less than 30 percent I always say five out of four people are bad at math so you know 30 ish make it all the way through that's the first thing I care about that's the first inflection point right like it's how many started how many ended that's the big picture a third to me I like that right I look at that and I think okay I'd want to know the next piece of data though of those people who see that so here here's the data that's missing that I would want to see to have a better understanding which is after that fourth page I assume there's an opt-in I would want to see the rate at which people opted in because the pieces of information I want are how many people start how many people become leads how many people see the last page how many people see the last page and then become leads and then the other crucial point is how many people go from first page to second page and half 54 percent went from the first page to the second page that tells me I see that is that you're doing a good job frankly now some people might look at that I think oh my you know 44 percent or 46 percent of the people didn't make it past the first page well people click on stuff all the time and bail so the fact that half the people who are seeing your first page and then it doesn't drop off precipitously after that right so it's a it's kind of a gentle curve you see in the in the middle there's that almost a little bit of a belly there that we I like to see which is you can't see my hands that I'm doing so I and what I mean is the middle is kind of flatter right it's not like a set of stairs where it's very dramatic each step I like to see the middle a little flatter where you're pulling them through the meter there's a little bit here so what can you do with this data but how do we use it so we could we could form some hypotheses so here's one hypothesis that I have which is that in general you're this is this is doing well it's doing what it's supposed to you're going to lose most people's attention if it's not a bleeding neck dire need for something that's just reality people are entertaining themselves and you've provided half of them a little squirted dopamine and a little bit of entertainment and that's fine and they've bailed they should most people you know aren't going to be interested in you're seeing that in the data so that doesn't bother me the question then becomes what what do we do with their interactions and what emerges from them at a point in time that those emergent properties so in this we can look at this and say like okay well this is a this is a tiny slice of data but when we different targeting of course but there there's so much nuance that that goes into this through this understanding of what you're doing that that and that's so important and and the beauty of what we're able to do but we're very close to the data like robin is close to his data is is robin is bringing that that's really the frame through which he's looking at the data we do that intuitively when it's our data we start thinking about who are these people because he knows how he targeted he knows from what's happening what the content is and that creates so much context and nuance and then it's just a you know and this is a fun exercise i did this with clients forever so i've i've practiced it a lot but it's a fun exercise to do for yourself which is write the story you think your data is telling like you're writing like you're explaining it to somebody else and write it as a narrative like and not you know like oh joe did such and such but it's okay well here's like here the main data points but then then you have to shift to it's like here the main data points here's the context here's what it means and what you'll find in that exercise and actually write it and for me my i would i would sort of share my you know framework my method but it's so it's so in elegant and so ridiculous and probably so useful only to me but i i i go on a treasure hunt when i used to do this with clients i would i would go on a treasure hunt and i would just want i just i loved i loved finding data so i would go and i would find data and i would i would just i would just scratch it down on pieces of paper or whatever like but i would see something that was interesting to me and then sometimes i may it might be a screenshot but i would assemble all of that and then try to figure out what does this mean and when i would sit down and write the narrative it when you're writing the narrative like literally typing it out to another person so for me it was typing it out to explain it to a client often to recommend a course of action so it was i had to make my case like this is what i think is happening this is why i think it's happening and because of that here's what i think we should do what you quickly realize when you do that is if you don't have all the information that becomes very clear very quickly but then you go look for it so if you say if you you think x is happening but you realize the data isn't really saying x you go look for something that would confirm or disconfirm that x is happening right so with this an example i might this is a not a great example but it would be sort of the method that if i were if robin said to me i really want you to figure out for me if is this working you know i'll give you access to whatever you need and then let's figure it out like i would probably start with this like okay well i would i would want to see the ads i just want to see the targeting i'm kind of understand who these people are and then i would understand their behavior and then i would expect and understand their behavior over time and then i would try to sit down and say hey robin here's what i think is happening you know here here's sort of the high level view of what's happening and here's what i think it means but somewhere in that very likely and you just heard me doing it as i was um you try as i was asking robin questions but at some point in that i would realize well i don't there are things that i don't i don't know and i need to know that before i can make an assessment or make any recommendation so then i have to go find out that information or find out that i don't have that information therefore i can't know that question without that information and it's just becomes this and this isn't something you do every 90 days or every month or frankly every week but it's something that you need to have as a a habit of thought a habit of work where you can at least periodically well early on you have to start laying the groundwork so you begin to get an understanding of like if you set up an ad for the targeting you know the targeting you're like okay great and then you begin to understand the ad performance like what's happening with the ad one of the comments like is it being shared um like like look at the stats okay you get a sense of that and then then what you're looking for when things are wildly different over time like you kind of get a sense for what each of the things it's doing and then it's just a matter of contrast but you do that for all the parts and that those parts are creating kind of a dynamic understanding that you're refining over and over and then you then you get and then there's the danger so that the benefit is that you do that and you start seeing insights because you're close to you understand how it's working the danger is you become blind to things you become blind to your assumptions in the intelligence community that the united states are something called red teaming it's it's definitely something that i recommend if you're working with clients uh clients sometimes are okay with this oftentimes don't want to pay for this but wish you're doing it with yourself red teaming is is i think very helpful and what what red teaming means in so the in the u.s intelligence community that produces intelligence assessments they're not finite known things i mean we're we're spying we're putting together human intelligence signals intelligence um you know all all kinds of forms of intelligence uh satellite imagery and we're trying to make a intelligence community to the best of its ability is trying to make an assessment like hey there are weapons of mass destruction in iraq like that was an intelligence community assessment what a red team does is come in and try to tear apart that assessment where can they find holes where's the data where's the conclusion not supported by the data where's the where are the conclusions um maybe not uh actually reflective of the data like whatever it is where are big assumptions being made so red teaming your own narrative is helpful too and you can some if you really want to have fun and you have a friend who is in the game too and wants to do it you can have somebody else help but it's actually much better if you learn to do it yourself because what will happen this is very much like writers who can edit as they write um you what you can do over time when i say over time this is six months eight months twelve months of habit in practice but over time you red team yourself in real time you'll you'll have an idea and you'll immediately challenge the idea and when you challenge the idea you'll go look for evidence that really is strong or you realize you don't have evidence that's strong and because of that you you sort of put that idea into this maybe category like you know this could be happening it just becomes a habit of thought um so that wasn't actually a question that was asked but it's it's something that i i highly recommend because the no one will ever understand your business as well as you do um that's just a simple fact i've worked with as i mentioned earlier over the 21 ish years um it was over 220 clients i never understood very well i want to be cautious about how they say this oftentimes i understood how their business was really functioning from the perspective that i saw better than they did only because they were blind to it um but what was you know also often true was that there were subtle nuances that only they knew that i just i didn't know the very subtle things that i mentioned that because you are the person who will best understand what your data is telling you and it should become part of the fabric of your dna that you just know it so when something happens that's odd that becomes really valuable to you you know you maybe you create a new ad and for whatever reason that new ad you express the value proposition differently and you got a of the amazon books called working backwards and they were talking about on the podcast i'll put the link inside the chat window there they were talking about how amazon how jeff bezos insists that power points are out and so they replace these 20 something page power points or these 100 page power points with a six page narrative that's it's law that's the only way that they do meetings um so the company people that are working in the company have have had to get really good at writing six page narratives because the narratives are far richer this you can you can you know you can you can see and experience all the nuance that that's happening whereas just looking at raw data you know everyone's looking at the same data but everyone's got the own narrative in their head and unless you as the person who's preparing this presentation um in amazon in this example is they're the one that actually has all the context so it's their job to create this six page uh narrative that everybody reads it so whenever they whenever they go into a meeting in amazon apparently for the first 15 minutes of a meeting or 10 minutes i forget how much it was it's just silence everyone's reading the six page narrative and then at some point everyone's read it and suddenly conversation happens and not everyone's on the same page and everybody can start to talk and and around that narrative that now makes clear sense to everybody in the same way so yeah that that reminded me of that podcast um i picked up the book but i've not read the section in the book about um i'm just looking at it now but i will make sense in a second so um i'm about 80% of the way through michael louis's new book called premonition i love michael louis as an author in premonition it dropped tuesday i think i can't put the damn book down but premonition is about the us in many ways the global response to um covid 19 and it's it's really it's frankly it's terrifying like if you had asked me what i thought happened with the us response i would have said well you know i assume there's some place in the federal government where we've been paying attention to pandemics for a long time we have a plan and it's the cdc and the national institutes of health and and what michael louis did is he found it as he generally does he just found really interesting characters but the reality of the us response to the to covet and really in many parts um the global response was that it it was this a very very small group of people back in the mid-2000s through through a chance through george bush reading a book about the 1918 influenza and realizing we didn't really have a pandemic response in the u.s government which is frightening and some very smart people from very disparate parts of the world not many of them not just in the government came together you know set up a seven total very a very small number and then put together through about it's it's such a fascinating story that it's worth reading just for the story but what the part that what andre was talking about that's amazing to me and the upshot here is that um it was it was really it was fewer than 10 people essentially saved humanity from an infinitely worse fate um one example of something in the book is that we the the conventional wisdom was that measures that we've been doing to slow the spread of of covet 19 like social distancing and closing schools and other things didn't work and that came from an analysis of the 1918 influenza that was flawed we looked at one city fence of philadelphia and those interventions happened a week before the peak it was too late but when they they did a retroactive analysis a small group of very smart people on their own time did a an analysis of the cities that weren't hit as hard what they found was that the social distancing and other similar interventions earlier reduced death rates by half or more so an insight that nobody else saw this small group of people saw they published it in academic papers other things but the part that andre just said that i thought was fascinating is that the there was an email chain beginning as a small group one of the members of that group happened this is a savant incredibly intelligent and who is intelligent who is looking at data coming out of china and translating data and looking at data that we had in the u.s early in the pandemic when the federal government was saying this is not going to be a big deal and and they he was projecting and they as a group were projecting the what if scenarios what if this actually you know these factors are true but all of that was done in narrative format all of this high level data of you know the r not of the virus the transmission rates the death rate the estimated death rates all of those things was a very very small group of people who were having conversations in zoom and others and but all but mostly it was driven by a narrative that one person actually understood what was about to happen and was trying to raise the alarm and was was using the narrative format to take the data and say and he had a metaphor that he used which is there was a tragic accident a tragic almost an accident but a tragic situation in the u.s with firefighters were killed the man gulch fire which was i can't remember where it was nevada or someplace but it was um firefighters parachuted in they went to fight if they had a plan to fight a fire when they got down there the fire was on the other side of the river and most of them died and and it's an example of what happens when a fire increases speed and power exponentially that a fire that is looks a long way away moving slowly as it gains exponential power it can be you know went from moving like 1.2 miles an hour 40 miles an hour in a very short period of time like these are all the things that context in narrative and metaphor convey and that is actually how we understand things i highly recommend the book just because every everybody should read it to realize how close we were to an infinitely worse disaster and how a very few number of people essentially saved us as a country and in many ways saved the world from a far worse fate um if you think that you're not you know if you have this idea that you can't make the difference read that book and you realize that a very small number of people um and individuals make enormous difference in the right situation but it also it's just an extension of andre's thinking about the power of a narrative the power of under actually understanding what's happening and putting it in some context and projecting out its implications like if this is true and this is true and this is true then this is likely to happen that is that's a superpower it's an absolute superpower so that'll be my closing thought for the day thanks as always to lauren for joining us on this excitement it's always always a pleasure thanks to andre of course for the opportunity to have a thinking out loud session and thanks to all of you who are on the live call today for submitting your questions and joining us for the fun right thanks everyone thanks lauren thank you
